channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran Ceasefire Crumbles; OpenAI and Anthropic Race to Deploy AI
item.description: The U.S.-Iran ceasefire is unraveling after American warships sank Iranian attack boats in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran struck the UAE for the first time since early April. Plus, OpenAI and Anthropic each unveiled billion-dollar enterprise AI ventures on the same day.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, May fifth, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The U.S.-Iran ceasefire is in serious trouble this morning. American warships sank Iranian attack boats in the Strait of Hormuz, Iran launched its first strike on the United Arab Emirates since early April, and Brent crude closed at its highest level of the entire conflict. We're going to walk through all of it.

ANDREW: And in AI, OpenAI and Anthropic each announced billion-dollar enterprise deployment ventures — on the very same day. We'll get into what that race actually means.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets from Friday. The S and P 500 closed near 7,230, gaining about three-tenths of a percent, according to CNBC. The Nasdaq touched a fresh all-time record, closing just above 25,100, up nearly nine-tenths of a percent. The Dow slipped about 153 points — a third of a percent — to settle just under 49,500. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.43 percent this morning. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate averaged 6.30 percent in Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey, published last Thursday. Mortgage News Daily's daily tracking had rates moving higher Monday, toward 6.56 percent, as the Hormuz escalation rattled bond markets.

ANDREW: Let's start with the weekend's dominant story — because as of this morning, the ceasefire that was supposed to end the U.S.-Iran conflict appears to be unraveling.

ANDREW: Here is the sequence of events. Sunday evening, President Trump announced what he called Project Freedom — a U.S. military escort initiative to physically guide commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has effectively blockaded since the war began in February. Iran immediately called that move a ceasefire violation. Then on Monday, the shooting started. The U.S. military said it sank roughly six Iranian attack boats that were targeting vessels under American protection. Iran responded with cruise missiles, drones, and small boats against U.S. Navy ships. And for the first time since the ceasefire took hold in early April, Iran struck a U.S. ally — the United Arab Emirates. CBS News reports the UAE Defense Ministry confirmed it intercepted fifteen missiles and four drones. One drone got through, igniting a fire at the Fujairah oil facility — a major regional fuel storage hub — and wounding three workers there.

JENNY: Wait — Trump had said the conflict was essentially over. Does this unwind that?

ANDREW: That is the exact question reporters pressed him on Monday, and he wouldn't give a direct answer. He said Iran had taken some shots but caused little damage. The Irish Times called the ceasefire all but over. CNN's framing was fragile ceasefire tested. Iran's argument is that Project Freedom itself is a provocation — that escorting ships through what Tehran considers its maritime enforcement zone violates the terms of the April eighth deal. The scale of the operation matters here: Project Freedom involves guided-missile destroyers, more than a hundred land and sea-based aircraft, multi-domain unmanned platforms, and about fifteen thousand service members. Iran's position is that that's not neutrality.

JENNY: And that's all showing up immediately in oil.

ANDREW: Significantly. Brent crude settled Monday at roughly a hundred and fourteen dollars a barrel — the highest closing of the year. It had briefly traded above that intraday. Overnight it pulled back slightly toward 113. For context, shipping through the strait was already devastated before this weekend — about five percent of pre-conflict traffic. S and P Global Market Intelligence counted just four ships crossing on the first day of Project Freedom. The IEA had already called this conflict the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. This weekend's escalation makes that characterization harder to challenge.

ANDREW: Two other national stories moving this week. First, the economy. The Bureau of Economic Analysis released the Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate last Thursday. The economy grew at a two-percent annualized rate — a genuine rebound from the half-percent reading in Q4 of 2025. But it missed the consensus forecast of around 2.3 percent, and the composition matters: investment and exports carried it, residential construction was a drag, and consumer spending held up. That last point is the one to watch. With oil now a fresh headwind on top of tariff uncertainty, whether consumers hold in Q2 is the central economic question heading into summer.

JENNY: And the Fed chair situation is coming to a head this week.

ANDREW: The Senate floor vote on Kevin Warsh for Fed chair is expected the week of May eleventh — days before Jerome Powell's term expires May fifteenth. The Banking Committee advanced Warsh thirteen to eleven on April twenty-ninth — the first fully party-line committee vote on a Fed chair nominee in the institution's history. Republicans hold 53 seats, so only two defections can sink it. One to watch: Pennsylvania Democrat John Fetterman has told Semafor he plans to vote yes, which would provide rare bipartisan cover. If Warsh is confirmed, he inherits the chair at an unusual moment — an eight-to-four-divided FOMC, an active war driving oil prices, and markets already pricing June rate cut odds in either direction. Jenny, speaking of forces shaping this economy — over to you on AI.

JENNY: Thanks, Andrew. Because Monday turned out to be a historic day in AI, separate from everything happening in the Strait.

JENNY: OpenAI and Anthropic each announced major enterprise deployment ventures. On the same day. Within hours of each other. OpenAI finalized what it is calling The Deployment Company — a ten-billion-dollar joint venture backed by TPG, Brookfield Asset Management, Advent, and Bain Capital, according to Bloomberg. The financial structure is unusual: the private equity investors receive a guaranteed return of 17.5 percent per year for five years. That is not normal venture math. That is a structured finance commitment from an operating company — and it signals how badly OpenAI wants PE distribution. The company is targeting four sectors specifically: health care, shipping, manufacturing, and finance.

ANDREW: What does The Deployment Company actually do?

JENNY: Its job is to push OpenAI's products deep inside the portfolio companies that its private equity partners own. Think about the leverage. A single firm like Brookfield or Bain may own dozens of companies. You partner with them and you get inside access to every one of those businesses as a potential customer. OpenAI controls the joint venture and is committing up to 1.5 billion dollars of its own capital.

JENNY: Anthropic moved almost simultaneously. It announced a 1.5-billion-dollar enterprise AI services venture backed by Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, Hellman and Friedman, and Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC, alongside Apollo, General Atlantic, and Sequoia. CNBC reports the deal puts Anthropic in direct competition with the world's largest consulting firms for corporate AI transformation work. The model is different from OpenAI's approach: where OpenAI is using PE portfolios as a distribution channel, Anthropic's venture is more engineering-intensive — embedding Claude specialists directly inside mid-sized businesses to redesign workflows around AI agents from the ground up.

ANDREW: So they're competing for the same enterprise customers but through different doors.

JENNY: Exactly. And the broader context is worth noting. Anthropic is simultaneously running its government lane — the Mythos cybersecurity model, the White House executive order discussions, the Pentagon relationship we've been tracking. The Goldman and Blackstone venture is a completely separate institutional track. Anthropic appears to be running multiple large institutional plays in parallel, which tells you something about the confidence coming off that 30-billion-dollar ARR number and the Google investment.

JENNY: Andrew, I know you've been watching what all of this means for the mortgage market. Let's go there.

ANDREW: The rate picture this week is genuinely complicated. Freddie Mac's PMMS for the week ending April thirtieth showed the thirty-year fixed at 6.30 percent — up seven basis points from 6.23 the prior week. That 6.23 reading was the lowest in three spring homebuying seasons, and it drove real buyer activity: the MBA's most recent weekly data showed purchase applications up ten percent year over year and refinance applications up 52 percent year over year. Buyers responded to that dip.

JENNY: And now that window may be closing.

ANDREW: That's the concern. Mortgage News Daily's daily tracking had rates moving toward 6.56 percent Monday as the Hormuz news hit bond markets. Lenders were already recommending locks heading into last week given the geopolitical uncertainty. That advice looks prescient this morning. The Fed held at three-and-a-half to three-and-three-quarters at its April twenty-ninth meeting, and with inflation risk back on the table via oil, there is no near-term rate-cut cushion to count on. The ten-year Treasury at 4.43 percent is the number to watch — mortgage rates tend to follow it closely, and any sustained move higher there will keep the thirty-year above 6.5 percent.

ANDREW: No major CFPB or FHFA regulatory news over the weekend. The action this week is in the data. The MBA's weekly applications report drops Wednesday morning — first real read on how buyers actually responded to last week's rate move and the weekend's geopolitical escalation. Jenny, back to Jacksonville?

JENNY: Let's do it.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 83 degrees and sunny conditions today. A good start to the week.

JENNY: On the local front, the Winn-Dixie headquarters incentive package remains in committee with no hearing date set. The twelve-million-dollar deal — a five-and-a-half million dollar revenue enhancement grant and a six-and-a-half million dollar headquarters retention grant — requires 200 new jobs at at least a hundred thousand dollars a year and a 65-million-dollar capital investment. The Jacksonville Daily Record has reported quiet concerns among some council members about the company's German ownership through Aldi. This deal is not yet through the finish line.

ANDREW: What is the redistricting situation doing to Jacksonville's maps? I saw the Supreme Court decision is now having local effects.

JENNY: It is, and it's moving fast. The Supreme Court's ruling in Louisiana versus Callais significantly limits how much race can factor into drawing voting districts. News Four Jax reports that Jacksonville City Council member Rory Diamond is now arguing the current Jacksonville council map is unconstitutional and must be redrawn. At the state level, the Florida Legislature passed new congressional district lines in a special session that could shift up to four U.S. House seats toward Republicans. Lawsuits are active from groups including Florida Rising against both the state and the city of Jacksonville. This is a slow burn, but it could directly affect local election maps later this cycle.

JENNY: Two quick updates on ongoing stories. The Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam Counties reached 80 percent containment at just under 4,800 acres as of late last week, with crews in mop-up operations. Today's dry and sunny conditions are a concern — firefighters are not ready to call it contained — and burn bans remain in effect across Duval and St. Johns Counties. Watch for a full containment announcement in coming days. And on the Jaguars: the 2026 home season opens at EverBank Stadium with reduced capacity of about 42,500 seats — the entire upper deck is closed for Stadium of the Future construction. For fans heading out to games this season, that is the experience. Construction remains on schedule for an August 2028 completion, with 2027 home games moving to Camping World Stadium in Orlando.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week: the Senate floor vote on Kevin Warsh for Fed chair, scheduled for the week of May eleventh. Jerome Powell's term as chair expires May fifteenth, and the Senate is running close on time. If Warsh is confirmed, the immediate market question is what signal he sends on rates — whether he accelerates the path to cuts that three FOMC members were already pushing for at the April meeting, or signals a harder inflation line given the oil shock. Watch for two things: Senate floor scheduling announcements in the next day or two, and whether any Republican senator publicly signals opposition. Two defections are all it takes to change the outcome.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday, May fifth. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Project Freedom Live, Supreme Court Guts VRA, Anthropic Frozen Out of Pentagon
item.description: A U.S. Navy escort operation is live in the Strait of Hormuz as Iran threatens to fire. The Supreme Court weakened the Voting Rights Act 6-3 last week. Anthropic hit a $1T secondary valuation while courts left the Pentagon's blacklist in place.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, May 4th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: As we record this, U.S. Navy vessels are in the Strait of Hormuz for the first time in an active escort role — a mission the Trump administration is calling Project Freedom. Iran has already threatened to fire on American forces that approach. We will tell you exactly what is happening and what to watch for today.

ANDREW: And the Supreme Court issued one of its most consequential rulings in decades last Wednesday — a six-to-three decision that effectively guts a key provision of the Voting Rights Act. Redistricting battles are already igniting across the South.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at Friday's close. The S and P 500 finished at 7,230, up about three-tenths of a percent, hitting another record. The Dow slipped 153 points to just under 49,500. The big move was in the Nasdaq — it crossed 25,000 for the first time in history, closing at 25,114, up nearly nine-tenths of a percent. Apple beat quarterly earnings and jumped about three percent, pulling the rest of mega-cap tech with it. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four-point-three-nine percent. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate moved to six-point-three percent, according to Freddie Mac's Thursday survey — up from six-point-two-three the week before.

ANDREW: The weekend in brief: first-quarter GDP came in at two percent annualized on Thursday, the Supreme Court struck a major blow to the Voting Rights Act last Wednesday, and Sunday evening Trump announced the Hormuz escort operation that launched this morning. National is where we start.

ANDREW: On Project Freedom — the operation launched early this morning. At its core, it is the first active U.S. military effort to physically escort commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz since the Iran conflict began in late February. The scale is significant: roughly fifteen thousand service members, more than one hundred aircraft, and guided-missile destroyers. The humanitarian rationale is real. Hundreds of commercial vessels are stranded in the Persian Gulf — oil tankers, cargo ships, cruise ships — with crew members reporting dwindling supplies of food and drinking water. Trump announced the operation Sunday evening and said any interference will be, quote, dealt with forcefully.

JENNY: And Iran's response?

ANDREW: Immediate and hostile. Iran's military called it a ceasefire violation and warned that any American forces approaching the strait would be attacked. As of this recording, no convoy has attempted passage yet and no shots have been fired. The first attempt is expected later today. Every tanker that gets through has a direct line to oil prices — Brent crude is sitting around one hundred ten dollars a barrel right now. Gas nationally is running about four dollars and thirty-nine cents a gallon. The administration's theory is that Iran's blockade leverage evaporates the moment U.S. military presence makes the strait unenforceable. Whether that holds is the most consequential question in global markets today.

JENNY: And if Iran actually fires on a U.S. vessel?

ANDREW: Then every market and every diplomatic relationship in the region moves fast. The Pentagon has put the conflict's total cost at roughly twenty-five billion dollars so far, according to officials briefed on the military budget. We are watching this by the hour.

ANDREW: On the Voting Rights Act — the ruling came last Wednesday, six to three along ideological lines. The case was Louisiana versus Callais. At issue was Louisiana's redrawn congressional map, which had created a second majority-Black district. Justice Alito wrote the majority opinion, finding that using racial data as the predominant motivation for drawing those lines violates the Equal Protection Clause. The ruling weakens Section Two of the Voting Rights Act — the provision that has been used for decades to protect minority voters from being diluted out of political power.

JENNY: Walk me through the contradiction there — doesn't Section Two sometimes actually require majority-minority districts?

ANDREW: That is exactly the legal tension at the center of this. What the Court said, effectively, is that even VRA compliance cannot justify using race as the predominant factor when drawing a district. The ACLU and the Brennan Center are calling it the most consequential VRA ruling since Shelby County in 2013, which gutted the preclearance requirement. The practical fallout was swift — Louisiana suspended its May 16th primary the next day to allow the legislature time to redraw the map. Republicans in Alabama, Georgia, and South Carolina are already examining whether their own redistricting can benefit from the ruling ahead of November's midterms. This decision will shape the composition of Congress for the next cycle at minimum.

ANDREW: Briefly on GDP — the Bureau of Economic Analysis confirmed first-quarter growth at two percent annualized. That came in just below the consensus forecast of two-point-two percent, but it is a substantial improvement from the fourth quarter's revised half-percent. Investment and consumer spending drove the gain. Imports weighed on the headline as businesses frontloaded purchases ahead of anticipated tariff changes. I will connect the GDP number to what it means for the rate outlook in a moment.

JENNY: Andrew, let me take it from here on the tech side, because the biggest AI story of this weekend comes with a real irony at its center.

JENNY: On Friday, the Pentagon finalized classified artificial intelligence contracts with at least seven major companies — OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Oracle, Nvidia, and SpaceX. These deals expand AI access into sensitive Defense Department environments: intelligence analysis, logistics, large-scale classified data processing. The company that did not get a deal was Anthropic. The Pentagon has maintained a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic since March — a label historically reserved for companies tied to foreign adversaries, and the first time any American company has received it.

ANDREW: What is the actual sticking point?

JENNY: The contract language. The Defense Department wanted Anthropic to permit its Claude model to be used for, quote, all lawful purposes. Anthropic refused, arguing that language could authorize domestic mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons systems. Anthropic sued the administration. Courts have so far declined to block the Pentagon's designation, and the contracts with the other companies went out Friday anyway. Here is where it gets interesting: the same week Anthropic is being frozen out of government work, it is having a remarkable commercial run. Secondary market trading platforms are now pricing Anthropic above one trillion dollars in valuation — putting it ahead of OpenAI for the first time. And TechCrunch reported late last week that a formal funding round at over nine hundred billion dollars could close within two weeks.

ANDREW: So the company the Pentagon blacklisted might be the world's most valuable AI company by end of the month.

JENNY: That is the situation we are in. There is also a White House angle here — Trump met with Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei in April and called a deal, quote, possible. Chief of staff Susie Wiles was in that meeting. But the Pentagon moved before any executive-level resolution materialized. Whether the White House steps in to restore Anthropic's access to federal contracts is one of the bigger unresolved policy questions in AI right now.

JENNY: One more note from the tech world — a preprint circulating among emergency medicine researchers found that OpenAI's o1 model correctly identified the diagnosis for roughly 67 percent of emergency room patients when given electronic records and a brief nurse intake summary. Triage physicians in the same comparison were accurate in about 50 to 55 percent of cases. The paper is not yet peer reviewed, but multiple major health systems are already running parallel AI-assisted triage pilots. It is a signal that clinical AI has moved well past the proof-of-concept stage.

ANDREW: Good context, Jenny. Let me turn to the mortgage market, because rates had a telling week and the story behind the move connects to everything we have been talking about.

ANDREW: Freddie Mac's Thursday survey put the thirty-year fixed at six-point-three percent — up from six-point-two-three the previous week. That breaks three consecutive weeks of improvement, which had been the best spring rate window in three homebuying seasons. The uptick came from oil-driven pressure on the ten-year Treasury. The mechanism is direct: when Hormuz tensions rise, inflation expectations move, bond prices fall, yields rise, and mortgage rates follow within days. The Hormuz situation is not an abstraction for home buyers right now — it is showing up in their rate sheet.

JENNY: Does a seven-basis-point move actually change buyer behavior at the margin?

ANDREW: It takes some urgency off the table. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported that applications for the week ending April 24th were down about one-and-a-half percent. More revealing is the composition — adjustable-rate mortgages climbed to eight-point-three percent of total applications. That is buyers betting rates will fall later rather than locking a fixed rate now. They are hedging, which tells you the psychology is fragile. If Project Freedom succeeds and oil retreats, you could see the thirty-year pull back toward the low sixes relatively quickly. If Hormuz stays contested, that rate goes in the other direction.

ANDREW: The other factor shaping the rate outlook is the Fed transition. Kevin Warsh's nomination cleared the Senate Banking Committee thirteen to eleven on April 29th — every Republican for, every Democrat against, the most partisan Fed chair committee vote on record. The full Senate floor vote is expected the week of May 11th. Jerome Powell's term expires May 15th. Warsh will walk into the chair role on the other side of a live military operation, a spring housing market under oil pressure, and a GDP print that keeps the economy in a hold-steady zone. His first public statements on rate direction after confirmation will move markets. Lenders are already recommending buyers lock in now rather than wait to see what he signals.

JENNY: So buyers who have been waiting for rates to fall further may be waiting a bit longer than they hoped.

ANDREW: That is the responsible framing. And sellers who were thinking of holding inventory until rates dropped further may want to reconsider the timing.

JENNY: Let me bring you to Jacksonville. Weather-wise, it is a beautiful start to the week on the First Coast — 86 degrees today, low of 70 overnight, clear skies. A welcome reset after rain moved through late last week.

JENNY: The forward-looking story worth flagging is a sign of where downtown Jacksonville is heading. Whole Foods Market is opening a thirty-eight-thousand-square-foot store at 1 Riverside Avenue in the Brooklyn neighborhood on May 21st. Downtown grocery access has been a persistent sticking point in the city's urban core redevelopment push for years — cited in almost every conversation about whether downtown can actually attract residents, not just visitors. A premium national grocer committing to that corridor is a signal that foot traffic on Riverside Avenue has reached a real threshold. The store's design nods to the Florida Times-Union building that once stood on the site, and the product assortment features more than 800 local Florida items.

ANDREW: What is the broader context for that timing?

JENNY: It is almost too well timed. The Jacksonville Jazz Festival runs the same weekend — May 21st through May 24th, Memorial Day weekend — drawing tens of thousands of people into the core. The Jaguars stadium reconstruction is at fifteen percent complete, on track for an August 2028 finish. JTA investments in the corridor continue. When you add a national grocery anchor to that picture, you start to see downtown Jacksonville making a genuine argument for itself as a place to live. The Whole Foods opening is a data point in that argument.

JENNY: On a harder note for local wallets — Duval County gas prices hit four dollars and thirty cents a gallon last week, matching the four-year national high tied directly to the Hormuz disruption. Before the Iran conflict began in late February, the local average was around three dollars and fourteen cents. That is more than a dollar-per-gallon increase in about ten weeks, paid by every commuter, every delivery driver, and every small business running a fleet in Northeast Florida. Every tanker that gets through the strait today is a data point on when that number starts coming down.

JENNY: Two more quick items from the First Coast. Duval County's school bus stop-arm camera program is now issuing real fines — two hundred twenty-five dollars per violation. The warning period ended last week. Blanding Boulevard has been flagged as the highest-violation corridor in the county. If you drive that stretch during school hours, treat every school bus stop as a camera event, because it very likely is one. And updating a story we have been tracking — the Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam counties was at about 80 percent containment as of late last week, and the rain that moved through Thursday and Friday gave crews the moisture they had been waiting on for weeks. Full containment appears close.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today — and genuinely just today: the first convoy attempt through the Strait of Hormuz. Project Freedom is live. Fifteen thousand service members are in those waters right now. Iran has threatened to fire. The strait has been operating at a fraction of its normal capacity since the war began in February. If the first convoy gets through without incident, watch for an oil price pullback by end of day — which translates directly into the ten-year Treasury, into mortgage rates, and into what you pay at the pump here in Jacksonville. If there is an incident, you are looking at a fundamentally different week. Check U.S. Central Command's public channels and any major wire service for convoy updates this afternoon. This is as live as a story gets on a Monday morning.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran War Powers Clock Expires, 4.5% Inflation Jolts Mortgage Rates
item.description: Day 60 of the Iran war: Congress fell short on a War Powers resolution and the White House claims the law doesn't apply. Q1 GDP 2.0% but PCE inflation surged to 4.5%, sending mortgage rates spiking this morning.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, May 1, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Today marks the 60th day of the Iran conflict under the War Powers Act — the legal deadline that is supposed to force Congress to either authorize the war or bring the troops home. The Senate voted yesterday and came up short for the sixth time. We will tell you exactly where that constitutional standoff lands this morning.

ANDREW: And the economy put out a mixed report yesterday that is already moving mortgage rates. First-quarter GDP came in at two percent, which sounds decent — but buried in the same release was a four-and-a-half percent inflation reading that has rate desks scrambling this morning.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,209 yesterday, up one percent — its first ever close above 7,200 and a new all-time record. The Dow jumped nearly 800 points, closing at 49,652, up 1.6 percent. The Nasdaq finished at 24,892, up just under one percent. April goes out as the best month since 2020 for both indexes — the S and P gained more than 10 percent on the month, the Nasdaq more than 15 percent. The ten-year Treasury sits at 4.39 percent this morning. Freddie Mac's weekly survey released yesterday put the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate at 6.30 percent, up from 6.23 percent the week before. But Mortgage News Daily's daily tracker jumped to 6.50 percent this morning on yesterday's inflation data. We will get to what that means for buyers in a moment.

ANDREW: Now, to the story that made today's date significant before most of us were awake. The War Powers Act of 1973 gives a president 60 days to conduct military action before he either needs congressional authorization or must begin withdrawing. President Trump formally notified Congress of hostilities against Iran on March 2nd. That clock hits zero today.

JENNY: And the Senate tried to do something about it yesterday.

ANDREW: For the sixth time, they did. Democrats, joined by Republican Senators Susan Collins of Maine and Rand Paul of Kentucky, forced a vote on a War Powers Resolution that would have required the withdrawal of U.S. forces. It failed, 50 to 47 — well short of the 60 votes needed to clear a filibuster. What makes yesterday's vote notable is Collins. She had voted against every previous version of this resolution. The fact that she flipped signals that Republican consensus behind this war is not monolithic — though 47 Democrats and two Republicans still could not outmaneuver 47 Republicans who voted no.

JENNY: What is the White House saying today about the deadline itself?

ANDREW: Two things, and they point in different directions. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told senators Thursday that the April 17th ceasefire — quote — "pauses or stops" the sixty-day clock. And separately, White House officials have been arguing the war is already "terminated" under the president's own authority, which would render the deadline irrelevant. Constitutional law scholars are dismissing both arguments — the War Powers Act does not have a ceasefire-pause provision — and Senate Democrats are now actively exploring whether to sue the administration for violating federal law. It is a live constitutional confrontation with no clear precedent.

JENNY: And oil markets are pricing all of this uncertainty in real time.

ANDREW: Brent crude hit 126 dollars a barrel intraday Thursday before settling around 114. Gas at the pump is still running above four dollars a gallon nationally — among the highest levels in four years. The International Energy Agency has called this the largest supply disruption in the history of global oil markets. And those elevated energy costs have fed directly into the inflation data that dropped alongside GDP yesterday morning.

ANDREW: The Bureau of Economic Analysis released the advance estimate for first-quarter GDP Thursday at 8:30 Eastern. The headline was two percent annualized growth — a genuine rebound from the 0.5 percent contraction we saw in the fourth quarter of 2025, which was depressed by a 43-day government shutdown. Investment, exports, and consumer spending all contributed to the recovery.

JENNY: But there was a significant catch in that same release.

ANDREW: The personal consumption expenditures price index — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — came in at 4.5 percent annualized for the first quarter. A separate March PCE report released alongside the GDP data showed 3.5 percent year-over-year inflation — well above the Fed's 2 percent target. Core PCE, which strips out food and energy, was running at 4.3 percent for the quarter. Economists are pointing to three overlapping causes: tariff pass-through into consumer goods, continued energy price pressure from the Middle East, and supply-side disruptions that are pushing costs higher without generating excess demand. That combination — growth recovering but inflation re-accelerating — is what economists call stagflation-adjacent. The economy is still growing, unemployment is still relatively low, but the directional trend is uncomfortable.

JENNY: And Kevin Warsh is about to walk into the middle of all of this.

ANDREW: Right. Warsh's full Senate confirmation vote is expected the week of May 11th, days before Powell's term expires on May 15. The Senate Banking Committee advanced him 13 to 11 last Wednesday — the most partisan committee vote for a Fed chair in the modern era. The critical wildcard is Pennsylvania Democrat John Fetterman, who has told reporters he plans to vote in Warsh's favor. If Fetterman holds, Warsh can absorb two Republican defections and still be confirmed. But he now inherits a chairmanship with inflation at its highest quarterly rate in years, oil in crisis, and a divided FOMC. The debate he walked into during his confirmation hearings was largely theoretical. That debate is now very real.

JENNY: Andrew, those rate desks had a long Thursday night. What are you seeing this morning?

ANDREW: Mortgage News Daily's tracker opened this morning at 6.50 percent on the thirty-year fixed — up 12 basis points from Wednesday, driven almost entirely by yesterday's 4.5 percent inflation print. Freddie Mac's weekly survey, which captures the prior seven days, still shows 6.30 percent — the lowest in three spring homebuying seasons. That number is technically accurate for last week. The daily tracker is where rates live today.

JENNY: So a buyer locking in this morning is in a meaningfully different position than someone who locked last Friday.

ANDREW: Twenty basis points matters. On a 400,000 dollar mortgage, the difference between 6.30 and 6.50 percent adds roughly 50 dollars a month to the payment — and over 30 years, that is nearly 18,000 dollars. Lenders have been telling clients all spring: do not wait for a dip that may not come. The thesis for lower rates this year was that the Fed would cut, oil would stabilize, and the ten-year Treasury would drift down. Yesterday's PCE put a dent in that thesis.

ANDREW: The MBA's latest weekly applications data, covering the week ending April 24th, shows buyers are still in the market — purchase applications were up one percent for the week. But total applications fell 1.6 percent as refinances dropped four percent. Even so, refinance activity is running 51 percent above year-ago levels because millions of homeowners locked in above seven percent in 2023 and can still benefit from today's rates. The refi story is still intact. The rate-lock-now message is intensifying for buyers.

JENNY: What is the number to watch on the rate front going forward?

ANDREW: The April Consumer Price Index drops on May 14th. If that report confirms inflation is running hot — economists are flagging 3.5 percent year-over-year as the key threshold — a June rate cut comes fully off the table, rates stay above 6.5 percent through summer, and Warsh's first FOMC meeting in late June becomes one of the most closely watched in years. If it surprises to the downside, the door reopens.

JENNY: Speaking of numbers, let me bring you to the AI beat, where OpenAI dropped something notable yesterday.

JENNY: OpenAI released GPT-5.5, describing it as a meaningful step forward from the previous model in reasoning accuracy and instruction-following, while maintaining roughly the same response speed in real-world use. It is live on the API and rolling out to paid ChatGPT subscribers now. The company is reporting annualized revenue north of 25 billion dollars — up from about six billion at the end of 2024, a pace of growth that has Wall Street in active conversation about a possible IPO in the second half of this year, though OpenAI's own CFO has said the organization is not ready to go public by year-end. Anthropic is tracking slightly ahead on revenue at 30 billion in annualized run rate, with more than a thousand enterprise customers each spending at least a million dollars a year — a figure that doubled from 500 in under two months.

ANDREW: That is an extraordinary concentration of revenue in two companies very quickly.

JENNY: It is. And the government is watching both of them closely. Axios reported Thursday that the White House is still in what sources are calling the "table reads" phase of drafting an executive order that would reopen federal agencies to Anthropic's Mythos model. That is the model released earlier this month that demonstrated the ability to identify thousands of zero-day software vulnerabilities across major operating systems. The White House had initially blocked Mythos from federal use after the Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk — because Anthropic refused to ease restrictions on its models being used in domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons applications. The new draft guidance is trying to thread a needle: give agencies access to the capability without Anthropic walking back those restrictions.

ANDREW: So has the executive order been signed?

JENNY: Not yet. This week's sessions were described as "table reads" — the White House convening companies across multiple sectors to workshop the language. The sticking point is whether Anthropic's safety conditions survive the negotiation. If they do, it would be a meaningful precedent: a company successfully holding a line on AI use policy against direct government pressure, and getting access anyway because the capability is just too valuable to leave on the table.

JENNY: Let me bring it home. Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at 88 degrees and showers today — a 60 percent chance of rain with possible afternoon thunderstorms.

JENNY: That rain is significant. The Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam Counties has burned roughly 4,800 acres and is about 80 percent contained. Most evacuation orders have been lifted, but Duval and St. Johns County burn bans remain in effect. The Florida Forest Service has described this as the worst drought in the region since 1872. Earlier this week, volunteer firefighter James Kevin Crews died during mop-up operations near Hilliard — a reminder of what these crews have been managing for weeks. Today's storms could be the turning point. The Forest Service has been clear, though: the fire can continue smoldering underground in swampy terrain even after surface containment, so one day of thunderstorms does not reset three weeks of drought.

ANDREW: Is the residential threat to surrounding communities still active?

JENNY: The immediate threat has been significantly reduced with the evacuation orders lifted. The ongoing concern is the underground smoldering and the potential for the fire to rekindle before sustained moisture reaches the root systems. Firefighters have been digging physical fire lines to cut off the spread, which is slow, exhausting work. Full containment has not been declared, and the burn bans will stay in place until the Forest Service is confident the drought conditions have actually reversed.

JENNY: On the business front, the Winn-Dixie headquarters incentive package is moving through Jacksonville City Council on schedule. The resolution — a 12 million dollar package combining a headquarters retention grant and a property tax incentive — would require Winn-Dixie to create 200 new jobs at an average salary of roughly 100,000 dollars by December 2031 and invest 65 million dollars in capital. A full City Council meeting is set for next Wednesday, May 6th. We have been tracking some quiet council skepticism over Winn-Dixie's ownership by the German grocery giant Aldi — the question being whether Jacksonville taxpayers should backstop a European-owned company's U.S. headquarters. Next Wednesday's committee hearing is the first real public test of whether that skepticism has any organized backing or is just background noise.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch: the May 14th Consumer Price Index report. Yesterday's first-quarter PCE reading — 4.5 percent annualized, 3.5 percent year-over-year on the standalone March report — is the most important data point to land in this economy in months. The CPI on the 14th will tell us whether that acceleration was a one-quarter event driven by tariff pass-through and oil prices, or the beginning of a sustained shift back toward the inflation environment of 2022 and 2023. Economists are watching 3.5 percent year-over-year as the threshold: above it, a June rate cut is functionally off the table, mortgage rates stay at or above 6.5 percent through the summer, and Kevin Warsh begins his chairmanship facing the hardest possible opening scenario. Below it, the rate path reopens. Mark May 14th.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Friday, May 1st. Have a great weekend.

ANDREW: We'll see you Monday.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Powell's Final Stand, Fed's Most Divided Vote Since '92, Oil at a Wartime High
item.description: Jerome Powell chaired his last FOMC meeting as rates held 8-4 — the biggest split in 34 years. Kevin Warsh cleared the Senate Banking Committee in a historic party-line vote. And oil briefly hit $126 as Iran talks collapsed further.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, April 30, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Yesterday Jerome Powell chaired what may have been the most contentious Federal Reserve meeting in more than three decades — the board split eight to four on rates, the deepest divide since 1992 — and just hours earlier, the Senate Banking Committee sent his would-be successor to the full Senate in the first fully party-line vote on a Fed chair nominee in history.

ANDREW: And this morning Brent crude briefly touched 126 dollars a barrel as ceasefire talks with Iran broke down further — Trump is now publicly telling Tehran to quote, just give up — gas hit four dollars and thirty cents for a gallon of regular today, according to CNN, the highest price since July 2022.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed yesterday at 7,135, down just four one-hundredths of a percent — essentially flat. The Dow fell 280 points, or about six-tenths of a percent, settling at 48,861, taking the hardest hit as energy and consumer stocks absorbed surging oil costs. The Nasdaq eked out a tiny gain. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.43 percent this morning. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at 6.23 percent, according to Freddie Mac's most recent weekly survey — the lowest the rate has been in the last three spring homebuying seasons.

ANDREW: Let's start on the national desk, and the Federal Reserve made history yesterday — though not the kind of history anyone in the rate market was hoping for. The FOMC voted eight to four to hold its benchmark rate at three and a half to three and three-quarters percent. Four dissents at a single meeting has not happened since October 1992. And what makes this especially striking is that the dissenters were pulling in opposite directions. Three regional Fed presidents — Beth Hammack of Cleveland, Neel Kashkari of Minneapolis, and Lorie Logan of Dallas — wanted to strip the easing language from the statement entirely, signaling they have no interest in future rate cuts. On the other side, Governor Stephen Miran dissented in favor of actually cutting a quarter point right now. Bloomberg confirmed the breakdown. So you have hawks on one flank, a lone dove on the other, and the center holding together by eight votes.

JENNY: Andrew, what does that kind of split tell us about the Fed going into this transition?

ANDREW: It tells us there is no emerging consensus. The hawks are reading the oil situation exactly the way you would expect — 126-dollar Brent crude is the scenario they've been warning about for months, and it makes them very reluctant to signal any path to easing. The dove sees a softening labor market and slowing growth and thinks the window for a cut is now, before the economy tips further. Powell held the middle for the last time yesterday. Whoever chairs the next meeting — almost certainly Kevin Warsh — is going to inherit a divided room with an active debate about whether the word easing should even appear in the statement.

JENNY: And the committee moved on Warsh yesterday morning.

ANDREW: The Senate Banking Committee voted thirteen to eleven to advance his nomination, entirely along party lines. Not a single Democrat crossed over. Reuters and CBS News both confirmed the tally, and Reuters noted it is the first fully partisan committee vote on a Fed chair nominee in the panel's history. Republican Senator Thom Tillis, who had been threatening to block the nomination while the Justice Department investigated Powell, dropped that hold last week after the DOJ closed the probe. The full Senate floor vote is expected the week of May 11, which means Warsh could be confirmed before Powell's term expires on May 15. Powell himself described the handoff as, in his words, very normal, standard — though at his press conference he also said that Trump's legal pressure has left him no choice but to stay on the board as a governor once he steps down from the chair. He told reporters the Fed has resorted to the courts to protect its independence and has been, quote, successful so far, but acknowledged that fight is not over. It was a pointed thing to say at your own farewell.

JENNY: That's an understatement.

ANDREW: Now the other major national story today: oil. This is not background noise anymore — it is the dominant force in the economic picture right now. Brent crude briefly hit one hundred and twenty-six dollars and change this morning, the highest intraday level since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, before pulling back to around 116. The catalyst is the complete breakdown in ceasefire discussions. Trump told reporters he wants the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports to continue and urged Tehran to just give up. Iran's military says the country is still in a war situation. The Strait of Hormuz is processing roughly five to eight percent of its normal traffic — just seven vessels transiting in the past 24 hours, versus a pre-crisis average of about sixty ships per day. The IEA has formally called this the largest oil supply disruption in the history of the global energy market. That is not commentary; that is the agency's official finding.

JENNY: And American drivers are getting the bill.

ANDREW: Directly. Gas hit four dollars and thirty cents for a gallon of regular today, up seven cents in a single day — CNN reports that is the biggest one-day jump in six weeks and the highest price at the pump since July 2022. If Brent sustains above 110, that feeds into the May consumer price index, which then becomes the data that either vindicates the hawks on the FOMC or gives Warsh political cover to signal a cut later in the summer.

ANDREW: From the rate decision in Washington to what it means for anyone trying to close on a house — let's talk mortgage. The Fed's hold yesterday was fully priced in, but the eight-to-four split complicates the narrative for buyers.

JENNY: How does that play out at the closing table?

ANDREW: Buyers and their agents were looking for clarity. What they got instead is a Fed statement where three members wanted to remove even the forward guidance suggesting cuts might come. That hawkish signal, layered on top of oil-driven inflation risk, leaves very little for the rate market to work with on the downside. That said, six point two three percent from Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey is still the lowest we have seen in the last three spring homebuying seasons — and lenders are actively telling buyers to lock right now, because the ceiling risk from triple-digit oil is real and whatever floor exists may already be in.

JENNY: Are buyers still moving, given all this uncertainty?

ANDREW: The most recent hard data we have is from the Mortgage Bankers Association for the week ending April 17 — applications rose seven point nine percent, with the purchase index up ten percent. The average contract rate in that week had dropped to six point three five percent. The next MBA weekly report drops this morning, and it will be the first read on how buyers responded after the oil surge hit. Watch for whether the purchase index holds at that level. On the refinance side, volume was running fifty-two percent above the same week last year — that tells you how many homeowners locked in north of seven percent and are now watching every rate tick with a finger over the phone.

ANDREW: Jenny, what's happening in Jacksonville today?

JENNY: Thanks, Andrew. Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high of 91 degrees and scattered showers today — and those showers are very welcome news for Northeast Florida right now. For the past ten days, fire crews in Clay and Putnam counties have been battling the Railroad Fire, which has burned nearly 4,800 acres. As of the latest update, crews have it at 80 percent containment. But the Florida Forest Service has been warning the fire could smolder for weeks because the underlying drought is, in their assessment, the worst since 1872. Today's rain could be the turning point those exhausted crews have been waiting for.

ANDREW: Most of the evacuation orders have been lifted at this point, right?

JENNY: Most have, yes. Duval and St. Johns county burn bans remain in effect, and crews are still actively mopping up hot spots — looking for any visible smoke before they stand down. Even with today's rain, the broader fire risk in the region stays elevated as long as the drought conditions hold. And on a somber note this week: the community is mourning volunteer firefighter James Kevin Crews, who died after suffering a medical emergency while mopping up a brush fire in Hilliard. He was working to protect his neighbors when it happened.

JENNY: A couple of other things on the Jacksonville radar. The Jaguars finished the 2026 NFL Draft on Saturday with ten total picks over three days, including six on Day Three alone. The class addresses depth needs at edge and wide receiver, and analysts at Big Cat Country and Sports Illustrated both called it a solid haul going into training camp. And separately, one to watch at City Hall: the Winn-Dixie headquarters incentive package — a twelve-million-dollar deal split between a five-and-a-half-million-dollar recapture grant and a six-and-a-half-million-dollar headquarters retention grant — was formally introduced to City Council as Resolution 2026-326 on Monday night, confirmed by the Jacksonville Daily Record. The deal would keep Winn-Dixie's offices at its Edgewood Court campus and add two hundred jobs averaging one hundred thousand dollars in salary, with the company committed through 2031. It's headed to committee, so no vote yet. But expect that to be a conversation — Winn-Dixie is now owned by Aldi, and some council members are already quietly asking whether Jacksonville taxpayers should be cutting a check for a foreign-owned chain.

ANDREW: That twelve-million-dollar question is going to hang over committee for a while. For what it's worth, the Johnson and Johnson Vision Care incentive deal that went through City Council the same night passed sixteen to nothing.

JENNY: Right — that unanimous vote is both a blueprint for the Winn-Dixie supporters and a very high bar for anyone hoping to organize opposition.

JENNY: On the AI and technology front, the biggest story today connects directly to the Pentagon and the intelligence community. Axios and Bloomberg are both reporting that the White House is drafting guidance that would allow federal agencies to work with Anthropic again — specifically to onboard Mythos, the company's most powerful model yet. Here is the background: earlier this year, the Pentagon designated Anthropic as a supply-chain risk after the company declined to ease its restrictions on models being used for domestic mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons. The White House then ordered a governmentwide phaseout of Anthropic technology. What changed the calculus is Mythos. Over several weeks, Anthropic used Mythos to identify thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities — critical, unpatched security holes — across every major operating system and every major web browser. For cybersecurity and intelligence practitioners, that capability is significant enough that the White House is now workshopping how to bring Anthropic back in from the cold.

ANDREW: So this is essentially the administration trying to square the circle — they want the model, but they needed the company to hold a harder line on weapons use.

JENNY: Exactly. The White House is convening table reads this week with companies across sectors to draft an executive order that would walk back the OMB phaseout directive and create a structured pathway for agencies to onboard Mythos under specific conditions. For anyone tracking enterprise AI procurement, this is a significant signal about where the administration is drawing the line between safety restrictions it can live with and ones it considers unworkable.

JENNY: The other tech story this week: OpenAI formally ended its exclusivity arrangement with Microsoft on April 27. Microsoft had held first rights to OpenAI models since the original investment — that is now gone. TechCrunch and CNBC both confirmed the restructured deal makes the license non-exclusive through 2032, and Microsoft will no longer pay a revenue share going forward. Within 24 hours, OpenAI's models were live on Amazon Web Services. The read from analysts is that OpenAI is clearing the deck for a broader commercial push — and possibly a public market debut. Meanwhile, Elon Musk took the stand this week in a San Francisco trial over OpenAI's shift from nonprofit to a for-profit structure, testifying that Sam Altman and the board abandoned the company's founding mission. That legal battle has a long road ahead.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this morning: the Bureau of Economic Analysis releases its advance estimate of first-quarter 2026 gross domestic product at 8:30 Eastern — that is this morning. The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model had growth tracking at roughly 1.2 percent annualized as of yesterday, well below the 2.3 percent consensus in a Reuters survey of economists and a sharp deceleration from the fourth quarter. Here is why it matters beyond the headline: if the number comes in soft — anything below 1.5 percent — it hands the rate-cut faction on the FOMC real ammunition heading into June, and it puts pressure on Kevin Warsh to signal a rate timeline before he even takes the chair. If it beats expectations, the hawks who voted to strip the easing language yesterday will have a much stronger case by the time the June meeting rolls around. Either way, the first-quarter print is the first comprehensive economic report card for 2026 — and markets, the Fed, and the incoming chair will all be reading it this morning.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Wednesday. Have a great Wednesday.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Powell's Last Stand, Warsh on Deck, Brent Above 107
item.description: Powell holds his final FOMC meeting today as the Senate moves on Kevin Warsh, oil holds above $107 with Iran talks frozen, and mortgage rates flatten just above 6.1%.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, April 29th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Today is Jerome Powell's last FOMC meeting as Fed chair, and on the very same morning, the Senate Banking Committee is voting on his successor. We'll walk you through both.

ANDREW: And mortgage rates have flattened out just above six point one percent. We'll explain why that calm is fragile, with Brent crude still sitting above one hundred and seven dollars a barrel.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,138.80, down about half a percent. The Dow finished at 49,141.93, essentially flat. The Nasdaq was the laggard, down nine tenths of a percent to 24,663.80. The drag came from chip stocks after a report flagged growth concerns at OpenAI, and from another leg up in oil. The ten-year Treasury yield is sitting at 4.35 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking just above 6.1 percent according to Mortgage News Daily. Investors are essentially holding their breath ahead of the two p.m. Fed decision.

ANDREW: Let's start with the national desk, because today is a genuinely historic day at the Federal Reserve. At ten a.m. Eastern, the Senate Banking Committee will vote on Kevin Warsh's nomination to replace Jerome Powell as Fed chair. And at two p.m., Powell himself will deliver what is almost certainly his final FOMC decision before his term expires May 15th.

JENNY: So walk me through the morning vote first. We've been tracking this for weeks. Tillis dropped his block over the weekend after the DOJ closed the criminal probe of Powell. Is this a clean glide path now?

ANDREW: It's a glide path, but only just. Republicans hold the committee 13 to 11. Tillis's withdrawal means Warsh should clear, but a single defection deadlocks the panel. Democrats led by Elizabeth Warren are pushing back hard this week, accusing Warsh of evasive answers on the questions for the record. The Washington Post and CNBC both confirm the vote moves forward today. If it clears, the floor vote could come as early as next week, in time for Warsh to take over before Powell exits May 15th.

JENNY: And then Powell's last meeting this afternoon. What should listeners expect from the decision itself?

ANDREW: A hold. The CME FedWatch tool is showing a 100 percent probability the Fed keeps the target range at 3.5 to 3.75 percent. That would be the third straight pause this year. The reason is simple. Oil is up, inflation is at its highest in nearly two years, the labor market is sluggish, and the committee does not want to look reactive on Powell's way out. The interesting part won't be the decision. It will be Powell's tone in the press conference at 2:30 — what he says about the path forward, and how careful he is not to constrain Warsh.

JENNY: Speaking of oil — that ties directly to the other big national story, which is Iran.

ANDREW: It does. Brent closed Sunday above one hundred and seven dollars a barrel and held there into Monday. Trump said yesterday that Iran has told him the country is, quote, in a state of collapse, and is asking the U.S. to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. But the U.S. blockade is still in place, and Iran's latest proposal sidelines the nuclear question, which the White House is calling unacceptable. NPR and Al Jazeera both describe the diplomatic track as effectively frozen. Witkoff and Kushner's Pakistan trip got cancelled four days ago and there's no replacement on the calendar.

JENNY: So if there's no diplomatic track, what's the actual ceiling on oil from here?

ANDREW: That depends on whether the cease-fire formally collapses or just stays in this fragile limbo. Goldman has been calling it maritime trench warfare. As long as ships aren't transiting Hormuz at normal levels, supply stays disrupted, and any new incident — a mining boat, a tanker seizure — pushes Brent toward 110 quickly. The IEA called this the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. That is the inflation pressure Powell is staring at this afternoon.

JENNY: Andrew, that's the perfect segue. Take us into home lending — what does all of this mean for someone trying to close on a house this week?

ANDREW: It means today is a more important day for mortgage rates than most homebuyers realize. The thirty-year fixed is sitting just above 6.1 percent this morning. Yahoo Finance and Mortgage News Daily are both showing it stable. Optimal Blue has it at 6.26, Zillow's lender marketplace at 6.12 — call it the low 6.2s, basically. That's still the best spring window in three years.

JENNY: But you're saying don't celebrate yet?

ANDREW: I'm saying that calm is borrowed. Two things could disrupt it today. First, if Powell sounds even slightly more hawkish at 2:30 than the market expects, the ten-year Treasury moves up, and mortgage rates follow within hours. Second, if Brent crude pushes through 110 this week, we get another inflation print that forces rates higher into May. The MBA's most recent weekly survey, for the week ending April 17th, showed applications up 7.9 percent and the average contract rate down to 6.35. That was the rally. The new MBA print should land later this morning and that'll tell us whether last week's surge held up.

JENNY: For someone in the middle of a contract right now, what's the practical advice?

ANDREW: If you have a contract and a rate offer in hand, lock today, before the Fed decision. The downside of locking and being wrong is small. The downside of floating into a hawkish Powell or another oil spike is much bigger. We flagged this Monday and it remains the call.

JENNY: And the historical context here is worth saying out loud. Six point one is still elevated by historical standards, but it's the best spring rate we've seen in three years. Buyers have been waiting for this window since 2023.

ANDREW: That's exactly right. The refinance share of MBA applications was 44 percent last week, which tells you a meaningful number of homeowners locked in at seven and a half or higher in 2024 and are finally finding a number that pencils. If today's Fed message kicks rates back up to 6.5, that opportunity narrows fast.

ANDREW: One more on the mortgage beat. The CFPB and FHFA have both gone quiet this week — no major regulatory drops. Most of the housing finance world is just watching the Fed and the ten-year today. Jenny, over to you for the local desk.

JENNY: Thanks, Andrew. Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high of 70 degrees today with a low of 59. Easterly winds around 11 miles an hour. The National Weather Service is flagging a high rip current risk at Northeast Florida beaches because of that onshore flow, so if you're heading to the coast, be careful out there.

JENNY: Top local story this morning is the Railroad Fire. Crews from Clay and Putnam Fire Rescue and the Florida Forest Service have pushed containment to 80 percent as of yesterday, on a fire that's now burned 4,796 acres. That's up from 70 percent over the weekend. Calmer winds for a third straight day are giving firefighters the upper hand.

ANDREW: That's real progress from where we were last week. But officials have been warning the fire could smolder for weeks even after full containment is called. Are evacuations lifting?

JENNY: Most of the active evacuation orders have been pulled back, but the Duval and St. Johns burn bans are still in effect. And we're in what officials are calling the worst drought conditions since 1872, so even with the containment number ticking up, the broader fire risk for Northeast Florida hasn't gone away.

JENNY: On the business side. The Winn-Dixie headquarters incentive package — that's the 12 million dollar deal we've been tracking, with 5.5 million in a Recapture Enhanced Value Grant and 6.5 million for headquarters retention — was on the City Council agenda yesterday. That keeps Winn-Dixie at its Edgewood Court offices and adds 200 jobs at an average salary of around 100 thousand dollars, while retaining 500 existing positions. Council also gave final approval yesterday to a separate 12 million dollar incentive package for Johnson and Johnson Vision Care. That one passed 16 to nothing.

ANDREW: Two twelve-million-dollar incentive deals at the same meeting. Is anyone on the council pushing back on the precedent?

JENNY: Some quiet discomfort, particularly on the Winn-Dixie side, since the company is owned by Aldi and there's a question about whether public money should subsidize a foreign-owned grocery chain. But there's no organized opposition yet, and the J and J vote being unanimous tells you where the political wind is.

JENNY: One more local note. The Jaguars confirmed yesterday that 2027 home games will be played in Orlando at Camping World Stadium while EverBank goes through full renovation. Construction is now 15 percent complete and on track for August 2028. The 2026 capacity stays at 42,507 — about 27,000 seats offline, mostly in the upper bowl. Andrew, AI desk is yours — actually, mine. Let me take this one.

JENNY: To the AI beat. The big story continues to be the reshaping of the AI capital markets. Anthropic's secondary market valuation crossed one trillion dollars last week, on the back of Google's 40 billion dollar investment commitment announced Friday before last. Annualized revenue at Anthropic is now reportedly running at 30 billion dollars, up from about 9 billion at the end of last year.

ANDREW: That is a tripling in four months. Is that real ARR or aspirational?

JENNY: According to multiple outlets including TechCrunch and CNBC, that's the run rate as of end of March. Most of it is enterprise — large financial services and law firm deployments. The interesting wrinkle is that Polymarket is now pricing roughly 70 percent odds that Anthropic will be valued higher than OpenAI at some point this year. OpenAI's last round valued it at 852 billion. So we are watching, in real time, a possible flip of who the market sees as the leading frontier lab.

ANDREW: And on the open-source side?

JENNY: DeepSeek V4 dropped Friday before last. Two variants — V4-Pro at 1.6 trillion parameters and V4-Flash at 284 billion. Both are open-weight on Hugging Face, both have a one-million-token context window, and pricing is aggressive enough to undercut every major proprietary competitor. Reuters confirmed DeepSeek is treating it as a preview with no firm finalization date. The thing for our enterprise listeners to watch is whether U.S. banks or law firms actually pilot it, given the unresolved questions on Treasury and Commerce Department guidance for Chinese-origin AI inside U.S. enterprise environments.

ANDREW: And the OpenAI Codex update from this week — there was reporting that it's quietly displaced Claude Code as the leading autonomous coding agent. Is that real or marketing?

JENNY: It's a credible claim from independent reviewers, not just OpenAI's marketing. Worth watching. The frontier is moving in weeks, not quarters now.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today: the Fed's two p.m. statement and Powell's 2:30 press conference. The decision itself is locked in — it's a hold. What matters is the language. Watch for any softening on the path to the next cut, and watch the long end of the yield curve when Powell speaks. If the ten-year ticks above 4.4 percent on his words, mortgage rates move with it before tomorrow morning, and the spring buying window we've been talking about closes faster than expected.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Wednesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran Proposal, Powell's Last Fed Meeting, Winn-Dixie Vote, Brent Above 111
item.description: Trump weighs an Iranian Hormuz offer Rubio called unacceptable as Brent crude tops 111. The FOMC opens Powell's final meeting today, and Jacksonville's City Council takes up a 12 million dollar Winn-Dixie incentive package tonight.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, April 28th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: An Iranian offer to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is sitting on the President's desk this morning, but the Secretary of State has already called it unacceptable, and Brent crude has pushed past 111 dollars overnight.

ANDREW: And it's Jay Powell's last Federal Reserve meeting as chair, opening today, with markets pricing essentially a hundred percent chance of no move and everyone watching the press conference for what he says about oil-driven inflation.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed Monday at a fresh record of 7,174, up about a tenth of a percent. The Nasdaq also notched a record at 24,887, up two tenths. The Dow slipped 63 points to 49,168. The ten-year Treasury yield is sitting at 4.32 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking around 6.32 percent according to Mortgage News Daily. Equities are holding up despite the geopolitical noise, but oil is doing the talking. Brent broke above 111 dollars in early Tuesday trade, the highest level since March, which is what makes this Fed meeting so loaded.

ANDREW: And that's where we'll start. Jenny, the headline this morning out of the White House is that Trump's national security team is reviewing a fresh Iranian proposal. Tehran is offering to reopen the Strait of Hormuz if the U.S. lifts its naval blockade and ends the war, and Iran is willing to leave the nuclear program for a later phase of talks.

JENNY: That sounds like exactly the framework people have been hoping for. So why is Rubio publicly saying no?

ANDREW: Because the nuclear program is the entire reason the U.S. and Israel went to war on February 28th. Lifting the blockade and accepting a phased deal would, in the administration's view, hand Tehran a reset without resolving what they argue made the war necessary in the first place. Trump told reporters yesterday the U.S. has, quote, all the cards. So the calculation seems to be that more pressure will produce a better offer.

JENNY: And meanwhile, the cost of that pressure is showing up in oil.

ANDREW: That's the bind. Brent is pushing 112. WTI is around 96. And the United Nations Secretary General warned overnight that the standoff is risking a global food emergency, because shipping into the Gulf is still effectively blocked, which is choking grain and fertilizer flows out of the broader region. Updating the story we covered yesterday — Iran's foreign minister Araghchi is meeting Putin in St. Petersburg today, which suggests Tehran is trying to build leverage from the Russian side while Washington reviews the proposal. Goldman Sachs is now describing the situation as maritime trench warfare, and that framing tells you why the oil curve isn't pricing in a quick resolution.

JENNY: One more thing on the international front before we move on — the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire that got that three-week extension last week. That doesn't look like it's holding either.

ANDREW: It is technically still in effect, but Israeli strikes killed 14 people in Lebanon on Sunday, and the IDF says it hit roughly 20 Hezbollah sites in southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley on April 27th, which they describe as a response to Hezbollah rocket fire. The extension runs through mid-May, but the casualty math is making people skeptical it survives that long.

JENNY: All right, Andrew, with that backdrop — oil up, inflation pressure climbing, and the Fed gaveling in this morning — what's actually expected from the FOMC tomorrow?

ANDREW: Almost no chance of a move. Polymarket and CME FedWatch are both showing essentially a hundred percent probability the funds rate stays in the three and a half to three and three quarters range. March CPI hit 3.3 percent year over year, up sharply from 2.4 in February, and that was before oil broke 110. So a cut is off the table. The interesting part is the press conference at 2:30 Wednesday afternoon. This is Powell's last meeting before his term expires May 15th. Watch for how directly he addresses whether the oil shock is, in his framing, transitory or sticky. Markets will read that as a signal for the June meeting.

JENNY: And speaking of the next chair, the Warsh confirmation calendar is finally unstuck.

ANDREW: It is. The Senate Banking Committee votes on Kevin Warsh tomorrow morning at 10 a.m., and after Senator Tillis dropped his hold on Sunday, the math now looks straightforward — 13 Republicans, 11 Democrats on the committee. A floor vote is expected the week of May 11th, just before Powell's term ends. So we are likely to get the committee vote and the FOMC decision on the same day.

JENNY: That is a packed Wednesday. Let's stay on the rate story but pivot to housing, because the spring window is closing fast.

ANDREW: It is. Mortgage News Daily is showing the 30-year fixed at about 6.32 percent. Freddie Mac's PMMS for the week of April 23rd came in at 6.23 — that was the best spring reading in three years. The MBA's weekly applications data showed total volume up 7.9 percent for the week ending April 17th, with purchase applications up 10. But all of that data is from before oil moved above 105.

JENNY: So if I'm a buyer with a contract right now, what does this mean in practice?

ANDREW: It means the case for locking your rate today is stronger than it was last Friday. Mortgage rates track the ten-year Treasury, and the ten-year is being pulled in two directions — flight to safety pushing it down, inflation expectations pushing it up. With Brent above 111, the inflation side is winning. If oil stays here for another week, the consensus on the lender side is that the 30-year drifts toward 6.5 percent. Borrowers who locked at 6.23 last week look smart. Borrowers waiting for a five handle have probably missed it for now.

ANDREW: One regulatory note. Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac are quietly rolling back property insurance requirements for condos and single-family homes, accepting actual cash value coverage on roofs. That's a response to the homeowners insurance crisis, particularly in coastal markets. Florida, Louisiana, the Gulf Coast — this matters for closings.

JENNY: That is a real one for our local market. Let's stay on the rate watch but pivot to AI, because the story we've been tracking with Anthropic and Google has another wrinkle, and DeepSeek's V4 release is now showing up in actual deployments.

ANDREW: Take it away.

JENNY: So, just to recap for anyone who missed it — Google announced a 40 billion dollar investment in Anthropic on Friday. Ten billion now at a 380 billion dollar valuation. Thirty billion contingent on milestones. Five gigawatts of compute starting next year. And Anthropic's annualized revenue has gone from 1 billion at the end of 2024 to 9 billion at the end of 2025 to 30 billion as of early April. There's also reporting now that Anthropic is eyeing an IPO later this year, which would be one of the largest tech listings in years if it lands.

ANDREW: That's a remarkable curve. Where does DeepSeek fit?

JENNY: DeepSeek V4 launched the same day as the Google-Anthropic announcement, April 24th. One million token context window. MIT license. And it's priced at roughly one-sixth the cost of Claude Opus 4.7 or GPT 5.5 on enterprise-relevant agent and reasoning tasks. The early enterprise integrations are starting to surface. Aurora Mobile's GPTBots platform announced V4 integration on Friday. And — this is the interesting one — DeepSeek V4 is now compatible with Anthropic's own Claude Code tool.

ANDREW: So an open-source Chinese model, sitting inside the developer tooling of the lab Google just put 40 billion dollars into.

JENNY: Right. And it's why Treasury and Commerce are reportedly working on updated guidance for Chinese-origin AI in U.S. enterprise environments. For banks, law firms, anyone in financial services or housing finance, this is going to matter at the next renewal cycle. The cost gap is large enough that procurement teams are going to ask hard questions, and the answer is going to depend on whether Washington draws a clear line.

ANDREW: All right, let's bring it home. Jenny, this is your beat — what's happening in Jacksonville today?

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 82 degrees today with a low of 66 and partly sunny skies. Pleasant day, but if you're east of the city, you'll still smell smoke.

JENNY: Now, the big local story tonight — the Winn-Dixie incentive package, which we've been tracking. It hits the City Council floor for introduction this evening. Twelve million dollars total. Five and a half in a Recapture Enhanced Value Grant, six and a half in a Headquarters Retention Grant, in exchange for Winn-Dixie keeping its corporate headquarters at 5050 Edgewood Court and adding 200 jobs at an average salary of 100,000 dollars while retaining 500 existing positions.

ANDREW: I keep getting hung up on the optics here. Aldi is a private German grocery giant. They acquired Winn-Dixie last year. And Jacksonville is being asked to write them a 12 million dollar check?

JENNY: That's the political question. The Mayor's Budget Review Committee approved it unanimously on April 13th, so the administration is fully behind it. But council members I've talked to privately say there's discomfort with the precedent. Tonight is the introduction, not the floor vote — that comes later, after committee review. Watch for which council members publicly raise the precedent issue tonight, because that tells you whether this becomes a real fight.

ANDREW: And the Railroad Fire — what's the latest?

JENNY: A bit of good news. The fire is now over 4,400 acres but holding at 70 percent containment as of late last week, and rain is in the forecast for today, which fire officials had been waiting on for ten days. The Duval and St. Johns burn bans are still in effect. The drought is being called the worst since 1872. So even with rain today, officials are warning the fire could continue to smolder for weeks before they call full containment.

JENNY: Quickly, the Jaguars wrapped up their draft on Saturday. Tight end Nate Boerkircher in the second round at 56. Defensive tackle Albert Regis at 81. Guard Emmanuel Pregnon at 88. Plus six more picks across days two and three.

ANDREW: Did they fix the offensive line?

JENNY: Pregnon helps, and they used a fourth on a tackle. But the bigger Jaguars story is still the stadium — capacity for the 2026 season locked in at 42,507, down from 68,300, with the upper deck closed and two of the home games shifting to London.
